sourceMovie.title: Finding Dory
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Finding Dory for the same reason it broke through in 2016: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a animation rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Andrew Stanton keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The adventure and animation blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Moana
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Moana (2016) clearly understood what makes adventure work — the same quality that keeps Finding Dory rewatchable. Expect the same funny rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Super Mario Bros. Movie
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Super Mario Bros. Movie (2023) is the kind of film Finding Dory fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The adventure overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Ice Age: Dawn of the Dinosaurs
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Ice Age: Dawn of the Dinosaurs shares Finding Dory's gift for adventure — the 2009 film delivers animation without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Ice Age: Continental Drift
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Finding Dory compelling — the adventure, the animation — shows up in Ice Age: Continental Drift (2012) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that funny storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Inside Out 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The adventure energy of Finding Dory is alive in Inside Out 2 (2024), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a funny payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Penguins of Madagascar
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Penguins of Madagascar (2014) operates in the same adventure as Finding Dory, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The funny payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: The Lion King 1½
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Lion King 1½ (2004) belongs to the same conversation as Finding Dory — both anchor their adventure in animation emotion that doesn't let go. The funny undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Moana 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Finding Dory's adventure stayed with you, Moana 2 (2024) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The animation through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Zootopia 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Finding Dory excels in adventure, Zootopia 2 (2025) doubles down on animation to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Smurfs: The Lost Village
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Smurfs: The Lost Village (2017) is the natural next step after Finding Dory: same adventure DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and animation elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

faq.question: What should I watch after Finding Dory?
faq.answer: Try Moana, The Super Mario Bros. Movie, Ice Age: Dawn of the Dinosaurs—each shares DNA with Finding Dory while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Finding Dory?
faq.answer: Finding Dory (2016) is best described as Adventure, Animation, Family. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Finding Dory part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Finding Nemo Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
